title: Serum cPLI in Dogs with Acute Pancreatitis: A Prospective Study
abstract: Prospective observational study of canine acute pancreatitis using cPLI biomarker.
full_text_snippet:
Introduction

Acute pancreatitis is a common and serious condition in dogs. The condition involves the premature activation of digestive enzymes within the pancreatic acinar cells, leading to autodigestion, local inflammation, and systemic effects. Early diagnosis is critical to optimise outcomes. Serum canine pancreatic lipase immunoreactivity (cPLI) has emerged as the most specific and sensitive biomarker for diagnosing acute pancreatitis in dogs.

Methods

This was a prospective observational study conducted over 12 months at a veterinary teaching hospital. One hundred and fifty client-owned dogs presenting with acute abdominal syndrome were enrolled. Blood samples were collected prior to any treatment. Serum cPLI was measured using a validated ELISA assay. Dogs were classified into three groups: acute pancreatitis (AP), non-pancreatic acute abdomen (NPAA), and healthy controls.

Results

Serum cPLI concentrations were significantly elevated in the AP group (median 850 ug/L) compared to NPAA (median 120 ug/L) and controls (median 25 ug/L). A cut-off of 400 ug/L yielded sensitivity of 92% and specificity of 88%. The area under the ROC curve was 0.94. There was a significant positive correlation between cPLI concentration and severity of cranial abdominal pain.

Discussion

These findings confirm that serum cPLI is a reliable and highly specific biomarker for diagnosing acute pancreatitis in dogs presenting with acute abdominal syndrome. The 400 ug/L cut-off provides clinicians with a practical decision threshold. Future multi-centre studies should explore the utility of serial cPLI monitoring as a prognostic indicator during hospitalisation.

References

1. Steiner JM, et al. (2008) Development and validation of a radioimmunoassay for the measurement of canine pancreatic lipase immunoreactivity in serum. Am J Vet Res. 69(9):1174-1179.
2. Trivedi S, et al. (2011) Sensitivity and specificity of canine pancreas-specific lipase for diagnosing pancreatitis. J Vet Diagn Invest. 23(1):161-165.
3. Mansfield CS, et al. (2012) Specificity and sensitivity of serum canine pancreatic lipase immunoreactivity. J Vet Diagn Invest. 24(1):136-141.
4. McCord K, et al. (2012) A multi-institutional study evaluating the diagnostic utility of the Spec cPL and SNAP cPL in clinical acute pancreatitis in 84 dogs. J Vet Intern Med. 26(4):888-896.
5. Forman MA, et al. (2004) Evaluation of serum feline pancreatic lipase immunoreactivity and helical computed tomography versus conventional testing for the diagnosis of feline pancreatitis. J Vet Intern Med. 18(6):807-815.
expected_summary: This prospective study of 150 dogs with acute abdominal syndrome demonstrated that serum cPLI is a highly sensitive (92%) and specific (88%) biomarker for diagnosing acute pancreatitis, with an optimal diagnostic cut-off of 400 ug/L and strong correlation between cPLI levels and pain severity.
